html:
AICXF / 6345 JP: Aichi Corporation

AICXF is Aichi Corporation, a Japanese aerial work platform and specialized vehicle maker that screens worse than the actual setup. The old "cash hoard below book" thesis is now stale: after a large buyback and cancellation, Aichi trades around book, not far below it. The better thesis is different. Aichi has become a strategic infrastructure-equipment affiliate of Itochu, with a dominant domestic niche, a real services base, a 4% to 5% yield, and credible optionality that Itochu turns a mature manufacturer into a leasing, maintenance, used-equipment, and overseas distribution platform.

The live primary quote is about ¥1,359 on the Tokyo listing, or roughly $8.60 per AICXF equivalent at USD/JPY near 158. The OTC line is misleadingly illiquid: StockAnalysis shows AICXF last at $8.35 with average volume of only about 20 shares, so the Tokyo ordinary is the real reference market.

The forward valuation is no longer absurd, but it is still attractive if Itochu matters. At ¥1,359 and 64.57 million shares, market cap is roughly ¥88 billion. Aichi’s latest FY2026 results show ¥59.6 billion of sales, ¥7.5 billion of operating profit, ¥6.66 billion of net income, ¥26.7 billion of cash, almost no financial debt, and FY2027 guidance for ¥63.0 billion of sales, ¥7.9 billion of operating profit, ¥6.7 billion of net income, and ¥103.78 of EPS. That puts the stock near 13.1x forward earnings, about 7.8x cash-adjusted forward EBIT, and around 9x ex-cash forward earnings. The dividend is guided to ¥65, a 4.8% yield, with a 62.6% payout ratio. This is not a negative-EV gimmick, but it is still cheap for a protected industrial niche with a strategic buyer leaning in.

The key change is Itochu. In March 2025, Itochu agreed to buy a major Aichi stake from Toyota Industries and described the market as supported by Japanese infrastructure demand and overseas growth from maintenance, urbanization, and power/telecom buildout. Itochu also said Aichi has about 70% share in Japan’s truck-mounted aerial work platform market and laid out the important part of the plan: expand the value chain into finance, after-sales service, used-equipment sales, and overseas distribution. Itochu then increased its holding again, with April 2026 filings showing 32.88% ownership. That makes this less of a sleepy Toyota Group orphan and more of an Itochu-controlled industrial platform.

The market still seems to value Aichi as a low-growth equipment maker. That misses the asset-light extension. Aichi’s FY2026 service sales were ¥13.2 billion, up from ¥12.0 billion, roughly 22% of revenue. If Itochu can attach leasing, maintenance contracts, refurbishing, used-equipment resale, and overseas partner distribution to Aichi’s installed base, the earnings mix becomes less cyclical and more finance/service-heavy. That does not require a heroic factory expansion. It requires Itochu doing what trading houses do well: distribution, credit, customer networks, and balance-sheet intermediation.

The recent capital allocation is also better than the historical label. Aichi spent ¥12.8 billion buying back stock in FY2026, then canceled 10 million shares, equal to 13.4% of the prior share count. Cash fell from ¥46.9 billion to ¥26.7 billion, but that is the point. The cash was not just hoarded. It was converted into per-share value, and the dividend was raised again. The stock is not protected by a giant idle cash pile anymore, but it is protected by a clean balance sheet, a high payout, strategic ownership, and a domestic franchise that utilities, telecoms, railways, rental companies, and infrastructure contractors need.

The biggest fundamental blemish is cash conversion. FY2026 operating cash flow was only ¥0.8 billion, far below net income, because receivables rose, payables fell, and capex stepped up. If that persists, the thesis weakens sharply. The bull case needs working capital normalization and capex moving closer to maintenance levels, allowing free cash flow to recover toward ¥4 billion to ¥6 billion. The FY2027 earnings guide suggests management does not see an operating downturn, but investors should not pretend FY2026 free cash flow was good.

The other risk is that Itochu may be good for strategy but not necessarily for minority shareholders. At 32.88%, plus Toyota Industries still as a large holder, the float is tight and governance outcomes are controlled by strategic holders. That can create a scarcity premium, but it can also leave minorities waiting while Itochu extracts business benefits through alliance activity instead of a full tender offer. A buyout is upside optionality, not the base case.

The variant perception is simple: Aichi is no longer a pure TSE below-book cleanup story and no longer just a domestic aerial-work-platform manufacturer. It is a high-share Japanese infrastructure equipment company with a growing service layer, a proven capital-return reset, and Itochu actively building a downstream equipment ecosystem around it. The stock has already moved, but forward valuation has not moved beyond reason.

Bear case: $6.00, about 30% downside from the $8.60 Tokyo-equivalent price. Operating profit falls toward ¥6 billion, working capital remains poor, Itochu’s value-chain plan is slow, and the market values Aichi at 6x EBIT plus reduced net cash.

Base case: $11.50, about 34% upside. Operating profit grows to ¥9 billion over two to three years, free cash flow normalizes, dividends remain high, and the market gives Aichi 10x EBIT plus net cash for a better-quality service and equipment franchise.

Bull case: $18.50, about 115% upside. Itochu turns Aichi into a broader leasing, service, used-equipment, and overseas distribution platform; operating profit reaches ¥12 billion; the market values the business at 13x EBIT; and either further buybacks or strategic consolidation reduce the minority float.
